Image retrieval is the process of searching and obtaining images from a collection, which is crucial for curating exemplars to use as examples for segmentation.
The unlabelled image collection is mentioned in relation to diverse datasets, indicating that the collection may contain examples from these datasets for segmentation.
CLIP is a specific vision-language model mentioned in the context that is used for curating an archive of images for each concept of interest.
Text queries describing concepts are used with the CLIP model to produce embeddings for efficient image retrieval.
An archive of images is built by selecting the k nearest neighbour images for each concept, forming the basis for segmentation.
The mature approximate nearest neighbour search techniques are mentioned in the context in relation to efficient image retrieval for curating exemplars.
The mature approximate nearest neighbour search techniques [37] enable the readily application of the archive construction process to a large collection of images.
The modern vision-language models such as CLIP can construct archives of high purity, which means a high ratio of the archive images contain the concept of interest.
The use of co-segmentation enables the obtaining of concept segments from the images comprising the archive for a specific category.
Good seed pixels are ones that have close neighbours across each of the archive images, belong to the target category, and have consistent features produced by the visual backbone.
Pixels belonging to the same concept are used to construct a reference embedding for classification.
Pixels belonging to C can be classified using the reference embedding constructed.
Dense features are extracted for each image in the archive using a pre-trained image encoder.
Features are extracted for each image in the archive using a pre-trained image encoder.
Seed pixels help in constructing an adjacency matrix among all features in the archive.
An adjacency matrix is constructed among all features in the archive.
For each pixel, the similarity of its nearest neighbour among each image is identified.
The average support that each pixel has among the archive images is identified.
The matrix encodes the mean maximum similarity across k images.
Seed pixel locations are identified by applying an argmax operator to each of the k submatrices.
A classifier for concept c is constructed by averaging the embeddings of the k seed pixels from its archive.
The embeddings are L2-normalised to produce the reference embedding fc ∈ Rd.
Instances of the category in a new image xnew are localised by computing the dot-product between fc and the L2-normalised dense features Fnew.
An initial estimate of the probability map corresponds to category c is denoted by P cnew.
Dense visual CLIP features can be correlated against a given CLIP text embedding.
Regions of the input that are salient for the target category are highlighted by employing the DenseCLIP mechanism of Zhou et al.
Features Vnew ∈ Rev×h×w are extracted from the last self-attention layer values of the CLIP image encoder.
The features are projected into the joint space with the CLIP image encoder's final linear projection.
The features are used to compute the CLIP text embedding before producing a saliency map.
The CLIP text embedding is computed for the target concept and normalized before generating a saliency map.
The text embedding is applied as a 1x1 convolution to the visual features.
A sigmoid activation function is applied to the result obtained by applying the text embedding to the visual features.
The final probability map for a category is produced by the Hadamard product of the estimates.
All category prediction maps are concatenated and argmax is applied to the category dimension.

The language-guided co-segmentation is a component of the inference process that seeks to improve the quality of co-segmentation achieved.
The context elimination is another component of the inference process that aims to boost downstream segmentation performance by selecting common background concepts to be suppressed.

Integrating DenseCLIP into the inference procedure brings a significant gain in performance, driven by the robustness of CLIP features under zero-shot transfer.
Both language-guided co-segmentation and context elimination components further boost co-segmentation performance.
Applying a CRF as post-processing after context elimination results in a small gain in performance.
The mid-level categories used for evaluation in COCO-Stuff are abstract for retrieval, such as 'outdoor objects', which may include many low-level categories beyond the target hierarchy.

The low-level categories are directly used instead of mid-level categories in order to avoid ambiguity in the co-segmentation procedure, and then the predictions are merged into the mid-level categories.
Category names are rephrased to reduce ambiguity, such as 'parking' to 'parking lot' and 'vegetation' to 'tree' in Cityscapes and KITTI-STEP based on descriptions found in a specific reference.
